2023 · arXiv · Reasoning

Sparks of Artificial General Intelligence: Early experiments with GPT-4

A 155-page case study from Microsoft Research probing an early, text-only GPT-4 across math, coding, vision, medicine, law, and theory of mind — and arguing it "could reasonably be viewed as an early (yet still incomplete) version of an AGI system." The most cited, and most contested, evaluation in the LLM era.

Bubeck, Chandrasekaran, Eldan, Gehrke, Horvitz, Kamar, Lee, Lee, Li, Lundberg, Nori, Palangi, Ribeiro, Zhang · Microsoft Research
venue arXiv citations 4,227 topic Reasoning source ↗
// citation count as of June 2026
// the gap

What problem is this solving?

By early 2023 a new cohort of language models — GPT-4, ChatGPT, PaLM — was visibly doing things older systems could not, but the field had no good way to say what. The standard instrument, the benchmark leaderboard, had two failure modes the authors found disqualifying. First, the headline benchmarks were saturating: a single number on MMLU or HumanEval tells you a model is "good at the test," not what general competence it has. Second, and more corrosively, the authors argue you can no longer trust those numbers at all, because "we do not have access to the full details of its vast training data, [so] we have to assume that it has potentially seen every existing benchmark." They note GPT-4 even "knows the canary GUID from BIG-bench" — the secret string benchmarks embed precisely to detect contamination had itself been ingested.

So the paper sets a deliberately different goal: not to score GPT-4 on tasks, but to characterize whether it shows general intelligence in the broad sense psychologists use for humans — "the ability to reason, plan, solve problems, think abstractly, comprehend complex ideas, learn quickly and learn from experience." The gap they target is conceptual and methodological: how do you produce evidence about a system that may have memorized every test you could give it, and whose interesting property is breadth rather than any single skill? Their answer — case studies on novel, hard-to-memorize prompts — is itself the paper's most consequential, and most criticized, contribution.

// how it works

The method: probing, not scoring

This is not a model paper — the authors did not train GPT-4 and report no architecture or training details (the model is "purely a language model … using at its core a self-supervised objective of predicting the next word"). It is an evaluation methodology paper dressed as a tour. The investigative stance has four moving parts:

  • Phenomenological case studies across domains. Math, coding, vision (rendered indirectly, since this GPT-4 is text-only — it writes TikZ/SVG/JavaScript code that draws), medicine, law, psychology, music, and tool use. The claim is that competence spanning all of these in one model is the actual evidence of generality.
  • Adversarial, hard-to-memorize prompts. The signature example — "draw a unicorn in TikZ" — has no answer to retrieve; a model that produces a recognizable unicorn must compose a spatial concept it cannot see. The classic Sally-Anne false-belief test is "modernized to avoid the possibility the answer was memorized from the training data."
  • Side-by-side contrast. Nearly every probe runs GPT-4 against ChatGPT (text-davinci-003) on the identical prompt, so the reader sees the capability gap rather than an absolute score.
  • Deliberate hunt for failure. The authors "put special emphasis on discovering its limitations," dedicating Section 8 to a planning analysis they trace directly to the autoregressive objective below.
p(x1, …, xn) = ∏t p(xt | x1, …, xt−1) // the standard autoregressive factorization — what the paper describes in prose as "predicting the next word" (the formula is our gloss, not printed in the paper). Section 8 argues this strictly forward, left-to-right generation — no backtracking, no inner scratchpad — is why GPT-4 fails at tasks that require planning ahead, and why the authors float "the possible need for … a new paradigm that moves beyond next-word prediction."
// what they found

The results

The qualitative claim is sweeping: across every domain they probe, "GPT-4's performance is strikingly close to human-level performance, and often vastly surpasses prior models such as ChatGPT." Where the authors do put numbers down, the strongest results are in coding. On HumanEval, GPT-4 reaches 82% zero-shot pass@1, against 65% for text-davinci-003, 39% for Codex, and 30% for CODEGEN-16B.

HumanEval (zero-shot)pass@1
GPT-482%
text-davinci-00365%
Codex (code-davinci-002)39%
CODEGEN-16B30%

Crucially, the authors flag their own contamination worry — "it could be that GPT-4 has seen and memorized some (or all) of HumanEval" — and answer it with a fresh benchmark: 100 LeetCode problems posted after October 8, 2022, past GPT-4's pretraining cutoff, graded by the official online judge. Here the result is more sober. GPT-4's overall pass@1 of 38.0% is essentially tied with human contestants' 38.2% — comparable, not superhuman, once memorization is ruled out.

LeetCode (fresh, pass@1)EasyMed.HardOverall
GPT-468.240.010.738.0
text-davinci-00350.016.00.019.0
Human (LeetCode users)72.237.77.038.2

Beyond code, the breadth examples are the point. GPT-4 passes a modernized Sally-Anne false-belief test and reasons about the intentions and emotions of people in messy social scenarios (Figure 6.3). On a practical task — detecting personally identifiable information in text (on the TAB anonymization benchmark) — GPT-4 identifies all PII elements in a sentence correctly in 77.4% of cases versus 40.8% for Microsoft's purpose-built Presidio tool. It can also orchestrate tools it is merely told about, managing a user's calendar and email by emitting the right API calls.

And then the failures, which the authors foreground rather than hide. GPT-4 makes "extremely basic" arithmetic errors: asked for 7 * 4 + 8 * 8, it answers 88 (the correct answer is 92). Tested on 100 random single-digit-multiplication, two-digit-addition problems, it scores only 58% — "a task which an elementary school student … could solve." Section 8 documents systematic planning failures the authors attribute to autoregression: with no scratchpad and no ability to revise an earlier token, GPT-4 stumbles on problems that need working backward from a goal. Hallucination, both open- and closed-domain, rounds out the limitations.

// does it hold up?

The eval read

Sparks is the most influential and the most criticized evaluation of the LLM era, and the two facts are connected: it is an essay full of vivid existence proofs, not a measurement. Read as evaluation, four problems dominate.

The artifact is not reproducible — and not the model anyone can use. The paper studies "an early version of GPT-4, when it was still in active development," accessed internally at Microsoft. There is no API to reproduce the transcripts, and the system was demonstrably changing during the study — the authors note GPT-4 "improved throughout our time testing it," and Figure 1.3 shows the unicorn growing more sophisticated over a month of training. The deployed GPT-4 behaves differently: the authors themselves note that the misinformation and harmful-content examples (Figures 9.1–9.3) were refused by the deployed model, which "either refused to generate responses due to ethical concerns or generated responses that are unlikely to create harm." The most alarming demonstrations are of a pre-alignment model the public never met.

The evidence is hand-picked. Almost every claim rests on a single, curated transcript chosen by the authors. There is no systematic sampling, no inter-rater grading, no error bars, and "human-level" is adjudicated by the authors' own judgment that an output looks good. Where they do sample (the 58% arithmetic test, the LeetCode set), the picture is markedly less triumphant — which suggests the qualitative wins are a best-case view.

Contamination is acknowledged but only controlled once. To their credit, the authors are explicit that they "have to assume that it has potentially seen every existing benchmark." But that admission undercuts most of the splashy results: HumanEval's 82% may be memorized. The LeetCode-fresh experiment is the paper's one real contamination control — and there GPT-4 is merely human-comparable. The honest reading is that the only clean number in the paper does not show superhuman coding.

The framing outruns the data, with a conflict of interest attached. "AGI" is asserted on a contested definition, and the headline impression is set by the title more than by any result. Microsoft is OpenAI's largest investor and commercial partner, so a 155-page argument that its partner's product is "an early form of AGI" carries an obvious incentive — a caveat the paper's hedging language ("sparks," "early yet incomplete") softens but does not remove.

baselines

Always the immediate predecessor (ChatGPT / text-davinci-003) and a couple of code models — fair as a "what changed" contrast, but there is no rigorous baseline for the central generality claim, which has no comparison point at all.

contamination

Conceded outright ("potentially seen every existing benchmark"; GPT-4 knows BIG-bench's canary GUID). Controlled in exactly one place — the post-cutoff LeetCode set — where GPT-4 only matches humans (38.0 vs 38.2).

what's missed

No way to separate generalization from memorization without training-set access; no independent graders; the qualitative transcripts are cherry-picked; and the studied model is a non-reproducible, pre-deployment, internally-changing checkpoint.

would raise confidence

Pre-registered prompt sets, blind independent raters, freshly-authored held-out tasks (like the LeetCode control) applied across all domains, and evaluation of the public, deployed model rather than an internal build.

// bottom line

An agenda-setting landmark whose breadth of demonstrations is genuinely striking — and whose evidentiary standard is anecdote. Its durable contribution is less "GPT-4 is AGI" than the problem it dramatized: when a model may have memorized every benchmark, leaderboards stop telling you what it can do. Sparks made that crisis vivid without solving it — which is exactly why the harder, contamination-resistant benchmarks that followed (OlympiadBench, MMMU, AGIEval, GPQA) cite it on their way to doing the measurement it skipped.

// takeaways

What to remember

  1. It reframed the question. Sparks moved the field from "what does GPT-4 score?" to "is this general intelligence?", using multi-domain case studies instead of leaderboard numbers — and made "is it AGI?" a mainstream research question rather than a fringe one.
  2. The strong evidence and the weak evidence point in different directions. The contamination-controlled LeetCode result (GPT-4 ≈ human on fresh problems) and the unicorn-in-TikZ world-model demo are compelling; the AGI thesis itself rests on cherry-picked transcripts of a non-reproducible, pre-deployment model that even refused some of the paper's own examples.
  3. Its real legacy is methodological pressure. By arguing benchmarks are saturated and contaminated, Sparks helped motivate the next generation of harder, leakage-resistant evaluations — OlympiadBench, MMMU, AGIEval — which now cite it while supplying the rigor it deliberately set aside.
